speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In this interview, Leo Guilfoy tells how Lumberjacks got nicknamed Tamarack Slim, Butterfly Pete, Midnight Joe, Wood -em -up George, Society Red, Silent Joe, Cold Ham to name a few. How the IWWs were blackballed by the company and fought by the association. How he refused to join the 4 Ls. About Spokane employment offices, faking cooking for a railroad camp and about Dick Farrell and Axl Anderson.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Remember what you forget, you forget. And then after that I got some other things to ask you about too, but you got a lot of names down. One is Pac -Sack Dick, and I want you to tell me what you remember about Pac -Sack Dick. Alright, now, alright, I thought of his name right then.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Alright, we had a preacher come to Bovill, I'm on that now, eh?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Alright, we had a preacher come to Bovill and he was quite a friend of the Lumberjacks. And he was a good guy, he kept his feet on earth, he doesn't take you up to heaven right away. And when he'd come to camp, he always packed his blankets on his back in the early days. And of course the company liked somebody like that to go amongst the Lumberjacks and all. And so they'd give word at the camps, or different camps, there are lots of camps around there. One camp in particular had 350 men in it.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I've told you about that one, haven't I? Which was the number of that camp?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: 35. And that had more men on it on wheels since the Panama Canal was built.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: More than any other camp?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: More than in the whole United States. You see, when the Panama Canal was built, you know, they were all on wheels, the camps was, on tracks. Well this was on the railroad track, that big dining room, you know, and bunk houses all on wheels. And they had 350 men. And that guy you're talking about, Axel Anderson, he was the push. He called the boss the push. He was the push at Camp 35. Well tell me about... That was between Bowell and Clarkie. Bowell and Clarkie. Tell me about Paxac Dick and then tell me... Alright. Well, Paxac Dick, he had been a prize fighter, clean prize fighter. And he'd then studied ministry and then he became a lumberjack preacher. That's what we call him, Paxac Dick, because he's going to camp with a Paxac on his back like you come in today in here. And the gentleman actually liked it.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, when did he go to camp?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: The foreman was average orders from headquarters to treat him right. Those days, they had a, they call it the blue room. I suppose in reference to the blue room in Washington DC in the White House, they had the blue room there and the guests would stay in the blue room. And then at the one table there for foreman and guests, that would be in the dining room. Well, Paxac Dick come and they bull cook. Now the bull cook is what we call a janitor today, you know. The bull cook, he got wood for the camp and went wood for the stoves and he kept the camp clean. That is the buildings clean inside. And he would go to the, Paxac Dick come in and he'd take him over to the blue room and Dick would say, no, I want to sleep in the bunkhouse with the lumberjacks. Alright. And when the time come to eat, which they fed really good, they put on wonderful at the table. So, and many, they'd say, well, they'd invite him to sit there with the foreman. No, he said, show me a place over there with the lumberjacks. And they liked him so much and they all dug down in the pockets and gave him something and he didn't ask him for anything. And then after, he was there quite a few years later and then he disappeared. And this Johnny Miller that wrote that book, when trees grow tall, he looked him up and he found out that he was dead. But he was a Methodist minister in one good sized church in Spokane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he come through the camps very often?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh yeah, because there have been a lot of camps showing his panhandle, played the whole panhandle. And round Bovill and Potlach was an awful lot of camps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did he have his, where would he hold his meetings?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: In the dining room. He talked to them nice way, you know, and he didn't pretend that they were all going to hell, something would be saved. And he was a reasonable guy. As I said, he had his feet right on the earth when he was telling them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It wasn't too hard about the sin in life and the lumberjack, huh?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No, because you know how those guys would go and a lot of them would stay in camp all year round and in the fall of the year they'd go in and spend the money. And he'd try to tell them the evils of spending money just for their own good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they call this guy Pack Sack?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: His name was Dick Ferrell, F -B -R -R -E -L. F -A -R -R -E -L. Dick Ferrell. And the church had, when Johnny Miller went up there, the church went all out with him to show him what he'd done and everything like that after he'd left and what he'd done for the lumberjacks. They had his record right down. I suppose when he'd go down to camp, see, he'd make his headquarters up there in Spokane and step out. And he was good talk and he was a prize fighter, had been. I suppose that'd be... It was no sissy. Yeah, so the guys respected him more for that. Oh yeah, and he respected them for what they do and everything like that. He passed by, there'd be a lot of curse words said and he didn't hear them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Camp 35 now?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Alright, Camp 35 was on wheels. It had 350 men there and Axl Anlison was pushed. Remember that. 350 men. And it was the biggest camp on wheels since they built the Panama Canal. The Panama Canal, of course, was bigger. That was all wheels, the Panama Canal, you know. They had sidetracks as they were going along and had all these laborers in there and artisans and everything like that on those camps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have a hard time spreading this? Did they have this camp all spread out so there was enough room for everybody?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh yes, well, one big bunkhouse he'd hold quite a bit and a big dining room, maybe another dining room at the other end. It was all packed together. You could run right through, like on a train. It was connected but the door was right on to the other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a boss was Axl? What kind of a boss?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah. He was alright. He was alright. A lot of stories told him about him. When the Second World War started, the Second World War, the men were scarce. Scarce as hen's teeth. And they had to have mancatchers out to get the men to come in. And on the job, the bosses and all that had to treat them right or they'd quit. Now I'm going to tell you something. No, go ahead. Alright. Well, maybe Axl, some guy who didn't do anything right and he'd bawl him out or say something to him that he didn't like and the guy would throw the PV down and he'd say, stick that up your butt. Well, Axl would say, I haven't got room. He said, I've got two loaders and a few other things up there and I've got no room for more. He speaks a little broken. That's a nice way to handle that. He handled that and he didn't get mad. And you know, they used to freeze the guys on the job. For instance, if a guy came out and went to work, the employment office would freeze him on that. He couldn't quit that job. Or else they wouldn't give him another job, see. So this guy, Axl would come and bawl this guy out for something. Well, this guy really said, you can't count me because I'm froze. No, he said, you're not. You're thawed out. He just thawed you out.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He just thawed you out. Well now, Axl was an assistant superintendent at one time. He was telling me about the strike. Yeah, well, I went through the strike by maybe as much as him. I worked for a cedar outfit, E .T. Chapin. And he was pretty good with the men, you know. They paid him a little better than other officers did. And nobody would join this. Well, anyhow, one thing bad against the IWW was the red flag. They used to have songs about the red flag.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And all the songs they had, they had a lot of parodies on them. Mostly all on these hymns, you know, salvation army, this and other places. If I could think of something, a thing you want. And they were against the flag and they were against religion too. But the things they struck for wasn't unreasonable. For instance, they wanted eight hours a day. And not carry any lousy bodies with them. Make the company supply them with their own blankets.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: They wouldn't have been so bad a gut further if they hadn't been against the flag and religion. When you say the red flag, you mean the... Well, you see, Russia wasn't Bolshevik or communist at that time because they hadn't had the revolution yet. They had the revolution in 1917 about... towards the end of 1917 is when they revolted. And when did the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When do you remember the IWW's first coming out into the country there?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, the IWW was probably...

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, let's... Ten years before that, about 1909 or 1910, I say. Because I wasn't around them very much because I didn't ever dream of belonging to them. They had two things that were really wrong if they left those two things out and really the only things they struck for was eight hours a day and don't carry any blankets. Of course, I suppose if you've got that, if you'd like to do now, go up a little higher next time. But just think, today, if that's all they struck for was eight hours a day and not carry any blankets. Well, do you know how they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: what they did in the camps? Did they try to organize the men? Did they try to get the men to belong?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, they did on some of them, but not all of them. There were lots of them like me. They were against them in a way on account of their ideas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they the ones that had those little cards, the IWW, the red cards?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah, the red cards. And they could travel as a hobo on a train with them. The Brotherhood of Trainwre... the brakemen recognized them. Not all the time, but... Those days, you know, you're beaten to freight. The brakemen would come around and...

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: What are you riding on?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Give them four bits or something like that. It was all right. I haven't got anything. Get out! Get out of the first stop. Get out. And some of them got brutal and kick you out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You're saying they used the IWW cards for the brakemen? Sometimes they'd accept that?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Sometimes they'd respect it.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Because I never used one. I wouldn't have one anyhow. Well, did you know, were many of the lumberjacks...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many lumberjacks belong back in those days?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, really, the local lumberjacks, no. A lot of them blow in. Of course, a lot of lumberjacks float all around the country, you know. I guess they had quite a membership now. And the record, or the full record, is in... In Pullman, in the State University, they turned it all over to them and they folded up.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I know guys that belonged... did belong to them, local guys. Boy, they got after them. Terrible. Not the Wobblies. They call them the Bobblies. Not the Bobblies, but the company. Blackballed them and everything else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they leave the country?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: They had to. They couldn't get work. Some of them with families.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: There was a Governor Alexander in there at the time. And the local authorities was rough on them. And in those days, the local authorities had to play with the company. I imagine the potlites were the biggest taxpayers there was, you know. And they played with the company more so.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And of course, actually, he was the company man. I worked for a little... and the outfit wasn't very little, but they didn't care about you because they paid good wages. They didn't bother us. I know one time, a house right close to where I walked uptown,

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: a Swede owned the house there, and the Wobblies stayed there, quite a few of them. And they stayed in another restaurant uptown. Well, in fact, they let that burn up and we could have put it out. Caught fire and burned up and we could have put it out. Because he played for the Wobblies much. They could eat there and all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which place was this?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Vernon... that Vernon something. He left and he went to Portland and started wrestling.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, who let it burn up? Was it just a company?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Alright, it caught fire. We had a local fire department. Everybody was fire department. And it tells you not to look for something about it. Alright, and they let it burn up. There was a superintendent there. And the cop from the bull, Pat Moon, he comes from the... The water got shut off.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: It's up to you to believe that it was shut off or else it accidentally shut itself off or broke down. But she burned to the ground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did Pat have to do with this? Who, Pat?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Pat Malone, yeah. He was the deputy sheriff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, but was he manning the hose?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh yeah, he was deputy sheriff, you know. And he was cop, police cop, city cop, deputy sheriff. And he also got so much a month from the potlice to go around all the buildings.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the idea was that it was maybe him that shut it off?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, of course they don't see it. Johnny didn't even tell about it in that book. He said it was mysterious how the water was shut off. Yeah, but he didn't say that it was seeing Johnny. He didn't say that it was anything about the unions. He said there was some disturbance but he didn't make that clear. No. That's where it was coming from. Of course, that caught fire accidentally, yeah. Nobody said it on fire, I know that. Well, was there any talk of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did this guy talk about revenge afterwards?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh no, no chance, sir.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, one man in town, one company in town, what are you going to do?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Everybody worked for the potlice with the exception of that little outfit I worked for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the town pretty split over the IWW?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I mean, there are people that really support him and people that disliked him is the feeling I get. Well, of course, naturally we didn't like him in a way, you know. We didn't want him in there, but we couldn't help him. Of course, the outfit I wore didn't make any difference to them. They never had any with them.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Of course, there was a lot of, like today in the unions, you know, a lot of radical ones. And everything that would happen, they'd blame it on them. I know they were making hay there and they had an engine using a gas engine and the night when they left they put a big tap over it, an oily tap, and that caught fire and they blamed that on them. It was accidental, they didn't. And they sent trucks and took them down to Boise. That ought to be in the state record. Took loads of them down to Boise.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Loads of the Mobblies? For what?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: They're being a Mobblies. Of course, those days and today are so much different. So much different. They had power. And these Mobblies was trying to make the power and it's a good job they didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that it was the Mobblies that caused the conditions to improve in the camps?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, as soon as they got them cleared out of there, they went back, they went to eight hours, which we didn't have before. They realized something was coming, but they didn't want some outfit like the Mobblies to be the one that would get benefits from it, or the one that would be given the credits for causing it. That's a job. They come down to eight hours a day, and they come down to blankets, sheets and blankets in the camps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about the strike itself?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Now it started in 17, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it 17 that the strike happened?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah, well, I was gone most of the time then. See, I enlisted right there, and I trained there at Port George Wright.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that after they started the strike?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah. And then I come into town to straighten things out. I was alone, just single, traveling around. I come to town to straighten things out. When I got off the train, boy, they had what they call some association there, and they would grab you right there and get off the train, ask you, where's your Wobbly and all that. So when someone would come over and say, that's good for you. Of course, they did the same thing then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was this? Is this in Bovill?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: In Bovill. Yeah. I got 24 hours off to come home. I wasn't married or anything like that. I never had any family there at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the association Wobbly or against the Wobbly?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, the association was against the Wobbly. Okay, that's what I thought. Oh, yeah. I'll think of it after a while. We joined some association.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the Four L's?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No, the Four L's hadn't started.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: The Four L's are Company Union, see?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And, what is it, Loyal Lumberjacks and Lumbermen or something. Loyal Legion. Loyal Legion, yeah. Well, that didn't last long because really, a Company Union isn't so hot. Because they're all for themselves. They tell the men what to do. And that's like today. Now a union now is something. I don't believe in the way the Hamilton's either. You take a look at the heads of the union, what you've got. Well, half of them in prison and everything like that and one murdered a whole family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they want you to join the Four L's?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah. When I came back after the war, I went to work at what we called Camp 10. And I got a job. That's the first cats that they used to skid logs, the potlatch. And I got a job swamping for a cat. And there was a guy there, he was a straw boss there, and he told me, he said, he was a walker for the Four L's. And he told me, he said, now we're having a meeting in the dining room at Camp 6, Camp 10. He says, well I want to see you there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you want me to see you for?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He says, I want to see you take a Four L button. Well, they had a great big button with L L L on it. Well, I said, Bill, I don't think you'll see me there. And I said, I just got out of the army and by golly, I want to be free. And when I buttoned, he didn't show up. Next morning he told me I didn't have to go to work. Well, fine, I said, it's all right with me. And Stanley Hodgman was superintendent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I was in camp and he said, Leo, what's the matter with you? Why don't you have work?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I told him, okay. And he gave me a better job. Because he was a lieutenant in the outfit I was in, Company D. And he gave me a better job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when this Bill came in that night, what are you doing hanging around camp?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Nothing, I said. Nothing. And just then Hodgman walked up to Stanley and he told him, he said, you shouldn't have talked like that to him. He said, don't force him to drive or anything. Let him pick if he wants to. Fine. If he don't, he's all right. And he's still going to work here. There was a lot of them against that 4L. And a lot of them thought the only reason they had to join it was because they had to hold their job down.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: But the Wobblies were no good in other places on the count what I'm telling you. Against the plague and against religion.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: So you think, and they had a lot of bums through at the head of it. And most of them a lot of flotuses. But I do know this. I know quite a few Wobblies that enlisted right away when war was declared.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: We had one guy, his name was White. I can't think of his first name. We call him Wobbly White. He joined up and he was picked out as the best specimen of a soldier there was. His height, weight, and activity and things like that. There was quite a few joined up by the way. Of course, the floating guy WW members were rotten.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say floating, you're thinking of the guys that rode the rails all the time and went from job to job? Is that what the other thing was?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, yeah. Well, the hobo. You used to ride the rails too. But there wasn't so many hobos joined the IWW. It was pretty strong in the woods up in this country.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And it would have been really no good if they had gotten power at all. It would have been no good for everybody. Well, the way I heard it was that once the conditions got improved... Right away. Once their aims were met, they disappeared. That's what I had heard. Well, they wouldn't have been away. Of course, the conditions, that wasn't much. You could ask for eight hours. Not carry a lot of your blankets. Well, some of the guys were patriotic. So, what you're saying is... Well, it stopped. I'm stopping to hesitate. Like I said, between the flag and religion, it stopped right there. And once they got away from that, the coast was all chalked up, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When the strike started, did the camp shut down all over the place?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Mostly all, yeah. Well, the first time I knew the camp, you know, where I was, we didn't have wobbly. Alright, I went downtown and they were all walking up the track from what they called Camp H. And from the other way, other camps. And they were singing those songs. Out in the bread line, watching the job sign. We are on the bum boys today. The employment office ships east and west. Jobs are quite skirting, none of the best. And they were all too, it's a religious song. Hallelujah, I'm a bum. Hallelujah, bum again. Hallelujah, give us a handout. To revive us again. Then another one about, they had pie in the sky. You'll get pie in the sky when you die. Holy rollers and jumpers and shout. Give your money to Jesus this day. You'll get pie in the sky when you die. They used to have all those songs, you know. And, don't wrote them down, giving books out with the songs on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel, did you dislike them at the time? Were some of your friends Wobblies then?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, no, they said no. You see, most of the friends I work with, we didn't have one Wobbly in the whole outfit. Not one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have much of a sense of humor?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh no. Well, in those songs, you know. They sound pretty funny. Yeah, but then, you know, there were, those days, you know, you used to have the Salvation Army on corners and everything like that. And in the street. And they congregate when they left these little towns and the camps. They'd get out in Spokane, down on a skid road.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: You know what a skid road is?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: They'd be on a skid road there all together. And there were quite a few bums amongst them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was the strike have anything to do with you deciding to go into the war?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh no, absolutely not. You just wanted to enlist and go over. And you were in a, well, let's hold that. I want to talk about that a little later, a little bit about the war in France. I want to ask you about some of these other characters, some more people, some more names, and how they got their names. One guy that I was wondering about is this Tamarack Slim.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the story on him?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Alright, now. Alright, Tamarack Slim. He was about six foot four. And he had about a 28th waist.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Big toy, like a Tamarack. Well, Tamarack Slim come in one time, going to town, and there was a traveling tailor would take your measure.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He'd take your measure, you know, for a suit, and he'd send back to Chicago, and he'd get that suit back, you know, nice suit of clothes, at $22 .50. Well, Tamarack Slim was going up to, wanted to go to a town someplace, and the guy measured Tamarack Slim. He measured his waistline and his inseam, his pants. And when they come back, when it got back to that tailor back in Chicago, well, he must have made a mistake. Well, I know there's no man like that. He must have made a mistake. And so they put the waistline, and changed the waistline with the pantsline. When Tamarack come back, they stuff you with the pants, they fitted him about his foot there, and the pants come to about up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, the pants came down about his knee?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah, they put, they said, oh man, don't make, he said we never had a, he must have made a mistake. And so they made the pants, but they thought he made a mistake. 28 waist, they put a 28 inseam or something like that. Or vice versa, see. His waist was big.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Come a little below his knees. Tamarack Slim. He had a name, Tamarack Slim. He'll say, preaching him out of the woods, Tamarack, you know, Tamarack, big, tall, Satan. Tamarack Slim. All right, Society Red.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You got that there?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Mm -hmm. Society Red. Wait, let me, let me change.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He didn't stand long for that. It was all right for the company, but they, they didn't want him to, I had lumberjacks written all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The men couldn't take the chuck, huh?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, it wasn't getting enough. Chuck. They used to feed wonderful in those camps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this, uh, Haydown Jack?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I don't know much about him, but, uh, he'd done a lot of farming, worked on farming every once in a while. And they used to have a saying, it's hay down time. And, and he bought that word, and so they gave him that word, hay down.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, here's a guy named, uh. Don't ask me that one broken ass Pete.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about him? How'd he get the name?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, funny. Dragged on his hip.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dragged on his hip, huh? That reminds me of, uh, what was his name? Shitty Shirt, uh, Bill? Who?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, yeah, sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his name? Shitty Shirt Bill?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No, it wasn't Bill. He got it down there. I remember you slipping a little. Yeah, it was Shitty Shirt Bill. Yeah, it is now, I remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How'd he get that name?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, come drunk in the camp, wanted to shed all over his shirt. And that stuck it right there. I told you about this, uh, chief of police there at Aropino. He called me over one time, said, hey, he said, I've got five names you didn't put down. And I looked at him, he's dead now, that chief of police. He got killed in the red. Wonderful guy, just fitted a town like Aropino. Really. And he had five names there, Broken Ass Pete, Broken Ass Pete, that was one of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Shitty Shirt Bill? I said, by God, I couldn't, what name?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, he said, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: There's five of them, and I forgot now what they were. But he had five on the list.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: That's a pretty good name, that one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about, uh, uh, the weasel?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: The weasel. Well, he would sneak round at night. He'd get up and sneak round, just the habit of his sneaking round. And he'd lay down under some of the bunk houses and sleep and then move to the other bunk house and sleep. Yeah. That's pretty funny. Yeah. Well, all of those were nicknamed for something that happened or something they did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about, um, Butterfly Pete?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Alright, Butterfly Pete, he followed the log drive quite a bit. And you know when I step on one log to the other, and he'd just jump on one log and on the other, cross all the others before he'd go on a small log. Just tap on it, be on the next log. Just flying on there like a butterfly. And I think, I don't know whether he's alive or not, but he's living in Seattle. And he used to do his work down on the Clearwater River. At the log drive, that's ended now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he the guy that used to sneak up on people too? Or sneak away from people? Or was that somebody else? Who's that?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: You mentioned somebody who used to have a, he'd take, he'd look around, he'd be gone. Oh yeah, I can't think of his name now, I'm forgetting them all. There was, um, Midnight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his other name?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Midnight. You got it down there. Yeah, you gotta find it though.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Let's see, Midnight Joe. Yeah. He drove him to the streets when he'd come to town, and I was still didn't walk up and down the street at Midnight. No, he didn't got that name Midnight, they kept him in camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then it was High Trussell, you got that name down? Yeah, what'd he do?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: These guys at one time had to go to work over a trestle. And, uh, High Trussell, he was nervous walking over to the train, a logging train went over it, you know. And high! No, he wasn't going to do that, so he went and got down instead of going across with the rest of them on the trestle. On the ties, you know. He went down and first thing you know he put it down in the river and they all let it get down and save him. So they call him High Trestle, Pete. No. Jack, I forgot. It was, uh, you just have High Trestle. Yeah, well High Trestle he called him. Because he was too chicken to walk across the trestle. Yeah, and he got down from there. He had to come down, push him, pull him out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Blue Pete?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: When it got the least bit cold, his face would get as blue as can be. There were quite a few Pete's. His face would get...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Black Russian?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, he was Russian. An awful dark for Russian. There you go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about sourdough?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Sourdough was a cook. And he made it, done a lot of his cooking. Made it with sourdough. Years ago all the bread and stuff like that was made of sourdough. And you make sourdough for a starter, you know. You can keep it. Keep adding flour and water to it and it will keep for the next batch instead of yeast. You use sourdough. Have it in a quark. And it's a starter for bread. I heard that that was real popular in camps. Oh, yeah, sure. You know, most of the camps had a baker and they never bought bread. The baker never bought bread. The camps never bought bread. They always had a baker. The second cook would bake. If you got cream...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Cream Puff Pee Wee?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about him?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Alright. He's quite a guy. He was up here not long ago and he's really going places now. He's selling cars and he's really going places. He comes and sees me. He's the one that had the second hand store in it. He told me about the store. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did he get his name?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Alright. He went out to this camp as a pastry cook. Or second cook. And the first thing he made a bunch of cream puffs. And I don't know what happened with the cream puffs. But you bite into them and that cream would squirt all over your face. You know, the first thing Lumberjack would grab for them, he'd squirt all over his face.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Bird's eye. That was an old saying. And it is today. Bird's eye. You done something just right and you hit the right place. Bird's eye. For instance, if you were born to a board, you wanted to come out right perfectly on the other side. You'd go, oh, bird's eye, you'd say. Come right straight too. And things he'd do, he'd always holler bird's eye.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he on the mark a lot of the time? Was he good? Or was that just making fun of him?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, that's it. I kept saying bird's eye, making fun of him. Ah. He used to say that himself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about broad -shouldered Tommy?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Okay. He didn't have a shoulder any lighter than a five -year -old kid. Just narrow shoulders. He'd come sloping down the sides. Broad -shouldered Tommy. I seen him in Blueston here a few years ago. Oh, I suppose he's gone. Gone up with the lumberjack stay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where's that?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Next to it, on one side of heaven.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They get their own side?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Not down there. They're up there. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about what?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: There wasn't very many bad lumberjacks, you know, like in crime or anything like that. They'd come to town and get drunk and all that.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: But there wasn't many robbers or anything like that, or murderers or anything like that amongst them. Well, they're a pretty good lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't travel around a lot though, did they?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Not very much. Well, they'd kick an ocean. If one place kind of slacked up, they'd go to another place. Not far.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this big island, Peat?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Alright. Up at North Florida Clearwater, and I think it's covered over now, is the Warshak Dam. The island is. And he had a place on the island there. Big island.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And he went up there and he started a little sawmill up there. And it'd begun for a few months. He'd come back and he said they stole everything from the sawmill. They even stole the pond that the logs floated in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he get a sawmill stole?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: A little one. They had a small sawmill and they left it, you know. Big island Peat. If there's any stories about these guys like that, just tell me. I remember that big island Peat. He had been in the army and he was trying to get a pension.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He couldn't get a pension somehow or another. Some of his papers was long. And so I took him down to this chief of police I'm telling you about. And Bill fingerprinted him and we sent the fingerprints in to the army. The marshal in D .C. and by golly, a little while after that he got the pension. Because they fingerprinted in World War I. That's the first time whoever fingerprinted was used in the army, in the navy, or what have you. They checked and found out that he wasn't in the army. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this patterfoot?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, he'd walk up and down and he'd generally have a loose sole on his shoe. Clap, clap, clap at night. He'd walk up and down. So they called him patterfoot. Wood him up George.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Alright. Years ago they'd haul logs by team, but skid by team. And they'd call the log wood. And he'd always want a big heavy load. And he'd tell them that he had to be cross hauling with the team, the logs up on the wagon. And he'd keep wanting more. Wood him up, wood him up more. Well, they'd say you got too much now. No, wood him up more. More wood. Try to get every last stick of good on there. Like these truckers do today, you know. They want to get every stick on, you know, to count.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Even go overweight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ski jumper?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah. Well, I didn't know him very well. And he was quite a jumper on skis. But one time he went out ski jumping and he'd come back and he never skied anymore. His face was all scratched right down there. And he never skied anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the banger kid?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He was from Bangor, Maine. And in the early days there was quite a few, you know, the white pine starting and river driving started in the United States. In Maine. They had lots of white pine and rivers. And they liked it in here when Dvorshak Dam was, before it was built. They used the river to plow the logs down. And old Bangor, Maine. There's quite a few came from Bangor, Maine up there. And they nicknamed him Bangor, Maine or the big guy from Maine. And quite a few first of the white pine came from Bangor, Maine to Michigan. Michigan to Minnesota and Minnesota to Idaho. You mean the guys would work across the... Well, you know, early days Maine was started. It was fairly well played off for a while. And then they jumped to Michigan where they had some pine left. And then it comes to the next one. Was Wisconsin. And then Idaho.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the Lumberjacks know that about the old history of logging that started in Maine and then went to Minnesota?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah. They brought it over here. You know the word PV.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what a PV is? A roll log and a cant hook?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: They...that's the word PV came from Maine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that many of the old Lumberjacks around here had come from the Midwest or from the East?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, a percentage from Maine and then quite a few from Minnesota and Wisconsin. Because that was the last jump was the white pine and then they moved over to Idaho. Idaho, this panhandle had lots of white pine. Beautiful white pine. And cedar and white pine. And those days white pine was the king of woods. They'd even leave red fir and white fir and tamarack in the woods and log the white pine and cedar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How bad did that 1910 fire hit the white pine in your country?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh boy, it was terrible. I shipped out to it from Spokane at the time, 1910. And I got to St. Mary's and the guy knew me in the Mary's and boy God, don't go out to that, they said. Well, I said, I've got to work. Well, come on, we've got a job in the sawmill for you. I think it was about two or three hundred burned up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you going out to work in the fire?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: In the fire, fighting fire, yeah. Well, that was a terrible one. But I didn't get out on it. I had the card, but it was shipped out there and there was a lot of, I don't know where it is now. That would be a quite a relic. It would ship you out from Spokane, you know. Didn't cost you anything to get you a job or anything like that. And it would ship you out on the train to the Mary's or whatever. It was in all directions.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that the first time you'd come out to this country in 1910?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh no, a little before that, about nineteen -five, six. Yeah, and that was in 1910, that fire. But there was quite a few fires. In those days, we had no way of fighting fire, all as we had with the grubble and shovel. To make trails around the fire. And of course, the men that lived around where the fire was, wasn't such an off big bunch. And they'd be all shoved into it right away. And they'd have to send us to Spokane for bush loads of them. And most of them was hobos. They didn't want the fire put out.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: There wasn't much work to do. And that was an awful job fighting in those days. It was terrible. Fire was terrible for you. Because it was hard, tough work. You had to dig a trench and then watch the wind fall down over that. And after the burn, quite a ways, you'd have to watch that. And you'd have to get in there with a crosscut saw or two of you, and get that smoke from sparks, and fall the trees so they'd fall into the fire. Watch the ones that were just about ready to fall and try to fall them into the fire. And that was a hot job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were fighting right up out of the fire?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah, I hope so, yeah. And you didn't want them to go over that trail. Of course, if you did, you'd have to build another one right around that and connect the trail that was left.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I suppose that there were men that got killed fighting those fires too. A lot of them. And a top fire was a son of a gun. If you get up on top, boy, you could run like a fast as a man could run. Everybody would run.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever have any close calls when you were fighting them?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, quite a few. I was in there once, and when a top fire come, of course we'd run all the directions as much as we could. And he'd just, eeee, cut the top. You'd lose your coat or anything else you had out there with you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of camps did they set up for the men fighting the fires?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Any kind of a camp. Tried to do anything. You can sleep on the ground if you want. But they tried to keep you fed, and that was kind of tough too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean getting supplies in there?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah, getting supplies.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the Spokane, what was it like there for a guy that wanted to work out in this country, let's say? How was that hiring done in Spokane back in the year?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, they, Spokane had a good many employment offices.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And they had big, great big blackboards the full length of their room, office.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Now don't tell that story while she's here. No, they, and the jobs, every job you could think of in the world. Work on the railroads, they'd ship you anywhere to the United States.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You'd have to buy a sugan, you know what a sugan is?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No, you don't. Alright, a sugan is a quilt. And you buy a sugan, and you go and apply for the job, the railroads shipped all over the place. In fact, when I took the homestead up, I went and registered in an employment office. And they shipped me out to close by where, of course I shipped out from Los Angeles, close by where my homestead was going to be. And I got off, and they wasn't going to let me off the train until I got to Tucson.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: So you said you were going to work, and they shipped you out, and then you got off at your homestead. Yeah, I hadn't filed on the homestead yet, see. But I went to work in the logging camp, in Blinds Flue, Oregon. And the timekeeper there, his brother was manager of the biggest building in Los Angeles at that time. The Los Angeles Investment Company, on 8th and Broadway. And he gave me a letter to his brother, because his brother was interested in homesteads there at Casa Grande. Then when I was there, there was 20 people in Casa Grande, now there's about 25 ,000. So I went out, and there was an adobe hotel. Well maybe I'm a chubby fellow. Oh, you can talk a little bit more about it. Alright, there was an adobe hotel, then I got off, oh, when we got to Casa Grande, there was a couple, I think it was a woman and daughter. And I told them, I said, they're getting off at Casa Grande. And I told them, I said, I want to get off third school. I said, but that's conductor, or brakeman there. Well, the woman said, you get between me and my daughter. So this suga I had, I just left and took it under the bench, you know, on the train. And I walked out like no one belongs to me. We got down, and just got 25 feet away from the train, and they, brakeman, hey, you got a little bit off of me, you got a little bit off. I said, listen, I'm off. Come and catch me. He couldn't run.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, why did a guy have to have a suga to get employed by the office? Because he showed he was going to have his bed roll with him?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah, sure, that was your bed roll. Because those jobs, you wouldn't have a bed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was there many guys in Spokane waiting for jobs?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, yeah, you could ship out any, you could ship back east to Minneapolis and back there. A job would cost you a dollar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who did you pay, the man that gave you, sent you out?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: The man that gave you the ticket, the employment of it. I'd heard that sometimes you had to kick back part of your first paycheck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, some of them did when it got, times got better, and the job was better, see what I mean?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Like, for instance, a cook that got, those days a good cook gets $75 a month. And, of course, he'd have to pay three or four dollars. Oh, they were crooked anyway, but these jobs just to travel, you don't want to pay a dollar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to pay for your train fare out there?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, no. Gosh, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they get, how did the railroad get paid for you?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: You were working for the railroad. Railroad had to have men on that job. Any job, you had a section, I never did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what a dandy dancer is?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Those days you'd tamp the ties with a shovel. Uh -huh. You'd tamp the ties and straighten up the rail. Section boss, you know, what the section boss was. He was a hard -boiled Irishman, you know, and he was a good man. You know, he had a hand car, push up and down, up and down. And the section boss would stand in the middle holding the handle. Well, he wouldn't work at all, just wanted to keep the balance on there. And there'd be about 40 on each end, pumping. All handwork.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did they call this guy Gandy Dancing, what was it, Gandy Dancing Tony?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Ah, Tony worked, the Gandy Dancer. And they got in with the Lumberjacks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I thought a lot of these railroad crews were Italian, right?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, everything. Some would have Italians, some of them wouldn't. They'd have everything you'd think of. And most, there was quite a few Italians, but not all of them. Austrians, we called them Bohunts. That's how we'd say it. And the Bohunts chucked the back in, and the Austrians in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get to know many of those guys that worked on the railroad?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No, because I never did Gandy Dancing. The only railroad work I did, I worked with the bridge and building outfit.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Out of Flagstaff, out of his own in there, on the Sound of Pee. I can tell you some good stories on the bridge and building, on the, ah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, there was a guy named, am I?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No, go ahead, tell me your story. Alright, there was a guy named Bob Caulfield.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was Manx, you know what Manx is?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah, Isle of Man. Well, Bob Caulfield and his wife, he was a push. And his wife and the cooking, good cook. And she took sick. And we were working on a trestle, lining the trestle up, you know. And putting braces and everything like that up under it. And putting the wooden trestle on the Sound of Pee. And he had to take his wife to town, to Albuquerque. We was close to Flagstaff. And he'd come round and he looked at all the guys, he said, he said, my wife's got plenty of all the meals ready. I want somebody to take charge here. Oh no, now, Gee, of course I wasn't so very old then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He looked at me, he said, what about you Leo?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I said, boy, I don't want to do any cooking. He said, you won't have to do much. And he walked up to the other, now he said, wait a minute. He says, let you guys vote on who will take it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, who got most of the votes?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Me. Well, I hadn't told Bob, I said, I'll do the best I can. He said, now my wife's got quite a bunch of stuff left. I said, I'll do the best I can.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And so, where? And I told him, I said, you tell these guys here, they've got some kicks on them, what you going to do to them when you come back?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, she had supper ready and they left on the train. The train comes through and starts to pick them up. And they went to Albuquerque and next morning, she had breakfast already, I could throw that on her. And next morning, knock, come on the courthouse door. I opened the door and here was a guy, a Mexican. He said, I have a chance for a handout. I said, come in. And he come in and I said, sure, I'll take your handout. Well, he said, if you'll show me what it is, he said, I won't trouble you, I can do it myself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I said, where are you going?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, he said, I'm going to be, he said, I cooked in the bigger hotel in Mexico City. He said, I'm trying to get to Phoenix, Arizona. And I've got a second job to cook in the Adams Hotel. I was in the Adams Hotel at that time. And he come in there and I said, boy, there's a chance for me right there. So I gave him a meal and I talked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, are you in a hurry to go to the Adams Hotel?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No, no hurry. And so I just got to thinking, I worked this guy in. I told him what I was going to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, will you join in? Yeah, he said, what is it?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, I says, you do all the cooking here, but don't let these guys see you when they come in for meals. The only thing they're going to say, make out is like me, they see. And I said, I know the engineer, his name is Walter Friend. And I've known him a long time and I'll get him to take you right to Phoenix on the train or on another train going down that way. And I said, now you can carry all the grub you want from here with you. Oh boy, he said, that'd be fine with me. So when they come in, they took lunch out and when they come in, that table was just growing with them. And one guy stepped in the door and he started to say something. I said, listen, you know, a cockle soldier, you shut up and keep your mouth shut and if you start kicking on this, you can come in and do that. No one said, we were trying to tell you how wonderful it was. They're not going to mind saying anything. Never mind saying anything, not at all bullcrap. And two days like that and breakfast in the morning, in the meantime, Friend had gone through, I'm afraid, and he come in, he'd always stop in. He found out I was there and he come in and I told him the deal and he laughed a bit and he says, you bet, we'll take that guy right to Phoenix. And I said, now you eat right here now, I'm the big meal writer and this evening when you're going through, you say the same way and they were coming back next day. Bob Caulk and his wife were coming back next day. And I told him, well, he said, I'll put on a wonderful breakfast. And Friend told me the time he's going through. He said, we're going to ride in the caboose in Royal, he said, and at the needles, California, he's going to put me on a train going to Phoenix. In the caboose. Well, gee, the breakfast next morning, oh, I was out of this world, you know, because he was a good cook. And Friend stopped there and rang the bell, you know, and got out, Grand Friend got off, you know, and come in and had a little lunch and he shook his guys on and he thought to conduct them, the conductor, he'd eat two meals from him already. And they would, oh, gee, they'd think he was a king or something. They walked him down the caboose and put him on there and put cushions on him in the caboose and off he went. And they came in about 11 o 'clock, Bob and his wife, whatever, you know, they put on a, and they came in about 8 o 'clock and, oh, no, we got to 10 o 'clock. And we saw the stuff that I hadn't cleaned off the table yet. And she says, they talked, I had a little brogue, those people, my little man. And she says, sure, you told me you couldn't cook. Well, I said, I didn't want anybody to know it. And when the guys come in, you know, oh, boy, they sure respected me. You bet they respected me. They thought I was wonderful, you know, to take that job, you're working on bridge construction and still cooking me like that.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Bean Ridge. Okay. He's still alive. Now, down here, there's a place called Bean Ridge. Near Troy. They raise a lot of beans there. And he come out to work in the logging camp, and that was Bean Ridge.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He's quite a guy. One time I was in Troy, no, Kendrick. And you got to have snooted up. I was sitting there eating and he come in there and standing in there, and he said, I can lick anybody that wears a sheepskin coat. And there was a guy in there with a sheepskin coat. And he stood up and he went outside and he beat the hell out of Bean Ridge. Well, they put him in jail and I come back there and I talked to the marshal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, he said, I said, well, what it cost to get him out?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Alright, he says, I wouldn't put no charge against him, but just for me picking him up, two and a half, and I paid him the two and a half. And he took ten Bean Ridge home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did Bean Ridge have to say about his boast? Not much, eh?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He said, I can lick anybody here with a sheepskin coat on. You know, if he's a sheep, I don't know. Were there many guys that came from that country, just local guys that came out to... There was quite a few. Well, a lot of lumberjacks, you know, like I tell you, me and Minnesota and that country, in the early days. And then afterwards they got native, born, and not only that, but lots of others, older ones, came in from Oregon. This is easier logging than Oregon, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is it easier?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, yes, smaller timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But isn't it more ridgy, up and down?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No, not like Oregon. That big timber's pretty tough. You carry those great big inch and a half chokers. You know, the choker is made out of cable. You'd be killed. You'd drag them up the hill. But here, as a rule, a half an inch, three -quarter inch is not heavy as a guard. And this is smaller timber around here, and there isn't a coast in Douglas -Furze or something. I worked there and it was a little... This Michigan bill, was he named that because he was... Alright, Michigan bill, I think he's dead, but he followed the log drive quite a bit. Michigan bill, if he followed the log drive, I knew him quite well, he followed the log drive. He would try to drink it. And then he'd get in the log drive, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this Hurry Up Jimmy?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Alright, he was kind of a foreman, and everything he'd do would be, Hey, hurry up there! Hurry up! Get that done, hurry up! Alright, hurry up Jimmy Sullivan. Get up there! We called him Hurry Up Jimmy.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He got back to the Wynton Lumber Company. They logged up here out of Bovill. They bought a bunch of timber, two thousand acres of timber from the potlatch.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I think most of the... I was wondering if most of the foremen were like that. I mean, most of them push him in as hard as they could. Well, in earlier days in my time, they were terrible. They'd come in and kick the bunkhouse door, and they'd roll out, roll out or roll up. We had a guy on there named Roll Out or Roll Up. Kick the door in, roll out or roll up. And they worked twelve hours a day. Of course, I'm talking about in Minnesota then, Michigan and Minnesota, they'd work ten, twelve hours a day. They got paid by the month. And they'd do that here before the... They had to buy their own tea.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: When they came out here, there were more independents, you know.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: They didn't bring those ways back from Minnesota with them. It stopped. It didn't go any further. But before the conditions got better, I would... Yeah, they did. They got better. The turn of the century got better. What do you think made a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there some foremen that were good foremen?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, of course, the later foremen were good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: The company made them good.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Because your man was quickened the whole time. He had to get another man. He had to learn the job. They found out. The company found out they were making money, so they canned the foremen. Tough foremen. It was something that got awful tough.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I suppose that if a guy got too tough, he just wasn't any good to have for the men. No, because he was changing the men and going around. And he would take a bunch of men that's dissatisfied and they ain't gonna do the work of a satisfied bunch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All right?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah, sure makes sense.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this Saginaw kid?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, he was from Saginaw, Michigan.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Saginaw was quite a lumbering town. If it jumped from Maine, it would come to Saginaw in that country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this galvanized Swede?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Okay, he was tougher than a son of a bitch. I figured he was as tough as galvanized steel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a big fighter?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, no. The Swedes, as a rule, didn't fight very much. But they would work hard to gain a position with the boss. The Swedes would. Yeah. I was trying to think of that other Swede there. Oh, boy. Steam shovel Ollie.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that down there?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about it?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Why, he...

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: They logged then with a steam range and all. They pulled the logs in with a steam rig and loaded the cars with the steam rig. Well, it had to have... come up on an apron, you know, and load the cars. And put another apron on it. And this steam shovel Ollie... Oh, he could work it. He'd get a great big shovel to level this off, put the apron on, for the rig to move on. Oh, he'd shovel it out in no time. The shovel was loaded up like that. So they attributed him to a steam shovel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were those guys stood out as being especially strong to the strongest men and lumberjacks around?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, yeah. Don't run away, Dorothy. Well, I'll come back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any guys that you remember when you were out there that really stood out as being real super strong men?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, they didn't show off. No, the lumberjacks didn't show off like, you know, didn't show this down. Because there were a lot of good men amongst them, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like Paul Bunyan did, you know him, don't you?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I've heard of him. Yeah. He did the things he did. He dug Lake Superior and he had a blue ox to really pull. There was a road there all curved. So he hooked his blue ox onto one end of it and dug his pee wee into the other end of it and the blue ox pulled straight in the road out. Oh, there's lots of stories about Paul Bunyan.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the lumberjacks used to tell stories about him in the camps?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, yeah. They'd laugh to be at the end about that. Paul Bunyan had his books out on Paul Bunyan.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they talk about him as back as early as World War I?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, way before that. Way before that. I guess that Paul Bunyan started in Maine years ago. You know, they'd tell about him getting on the round river.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And he'd get on the round river and he looked around and he said, Gee, that looked like the camp it just started from. And after a while, there was no round, it was just round the river. And he was taking a big raft of logs down and going round and round all the time on this river. And then he had a great big raft of logs. And he was taking them down to New Orleans. And he took the wrong logs and he had to bring them back.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, there's some great stories.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any other guys besides Paul Bunyan that the loggers used to tell stories about?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No, that's the only lumberjack. That was known as a superman. Yeah. And they have some great stories about him. I'd imagine that some of the local guys they must have thought were big drinkers or big fighters or something like that. Some of these guys that you're telling me about. Yeah. Well, you know, later years, they'd let a big roar out and there was always somebody to take them up. And they didn't fight much amongst themselves except when they were drunk. Really, there wasn't much crime amongst lumberjacks like there is some other occupations.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When they fought, did they fight real tough?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, yeah. And if they got a good chance, they'd stomp on you with their cork shoes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what a cork shoe is?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Spikes right in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I suppose that would finish the fight, huh?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If a guy got beaten, let's say, like that, would he hold the grudge bad or would he make up?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No. All the others would see he didn't hold the grudge. He'd generally leave right then.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: There wasn't such terrible fights amongst lumberjacks as it was. Most of you went to drink into something like that and then the city would take care of them.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I figured that there'd be grudges sometimes. You know, you take as a rule these other crimes, whether in cities and things like that, but the lumberjack works all summer amongst the timber and all that, beautiful scenery and stuff like that, amongst nature. It changes a man. He's not so mean. Oh, the lumberjacks thought a lot of nature and the trees and all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think even though they were cutting the trees down, they still really appreciated it?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this Russian Pete?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, he was just a Russian and Pete was his name. I used to talk a little bit of Russian to him. I'd get up there and when I was taking on this prison ship, you know, taking these Russian prisoners, bringing them back from Vladivostok in Siberia, brought them back to Odessa in the Black Sea, I learned a few songs. There's one there, This sweet dang yer, my lengthy my lie. This sweet dang yer, my lengthy my lie. You know, they've got a great big broad voice. That's goodbye, little girl, goodbye.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I go over to you, goodbye, and those were a fine bunch of Russians. Of course they were white Russians, see. That was before, quite a long time before the revolution. There was Kaiser, the Tsar Russians, and that Tsar was a cruel and his outfit. It was terrible. I seen some terrible things in Odessa when we landed these soldiers, these prisoners. Big fellas, great big guys. And the Japs were small.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean? What terrible things did you see?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, they were getting out bad on the Jews there then. And they'd kill the women and toss them out of the window, cut open. And one Russian told me, he said, I got a droski, you know, a droski's got the bells on it, and the ring. And this guy took me for a drive in a droski, the R -O -S -K -Y. And he showed me some terrible things. And he showed me where the Tsar's troops, the Corsics, they used lead on the end of their whips. They put down gatherings or riots with those. And they were, and buried right into here, points on the ends of these Russian whips.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they call them?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I can't think of them. I know what they called those, when they went after the Jews, they called those pogroms or something like that. But the Corsic was the main, the classic was loyal to the Tsar. Well, you see, the Tsar wasn't overthrown until 17. And then he switched right over. Well, there's no other way they could go. There was no, couldn't go any down any further. And half of the Russians that fought against the First World War against the Germans didn't have guns. They'd have to wait until the other guy got killed to pick the gun up.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: There probably weren't many Russians in the logging camps, son. This Russian... Not very many, no. You know, Russian is lousy with timber and white pine. I read a story where this flier flew over four hours over white pine. Take you ten minutes over this. Four hours over beautiful white pine. You know, the Japs is getting most of the logs out of this country now. And I know a guy that bought timber for two outfits, Evan's outfit and another outfit in Montana. Bought timber for them, and while he was here, they sent him, he's got an orofino, they sent him up to British Columbia, Vancouver, to take a look at some of this imported Japanese plywood. And I knew him well because he lived up in Bowell. And I seen him after a while and I asked him, I said, he's just got back. He said, Leo, he says, we don't know how to make plywood compared with them.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He said, they have something all engraved. He went to two or three big warehouses where ships had unloaded thousands and thousands of feet of that plywood. And oh, he said, it was beautiful. He said, no.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: They take the log over there and make it up and ship it and send it back here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And sell it as plywood?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Cheaper than we can, huh?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: It's cheap, anyhow, and that's much better. He told me that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is it that makes it better than ours?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, most of the, you know, we have two grades, indoor and outdoor. And it's indoor, they have to get a little bit wet. It swells up and the rosin quits. The rosin gives way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you say, Betty?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Hot.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Oh, is it hot?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He's the Dorothy coming back again. Well, let me ask you a few more of these guys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about this, is this right, Crooked Nick?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh yeah. Crooked Nick, he's got another name. There's a Crooked Nick. He's got another name, I can't think of it right now. Well, he had a Crooked Nick like that. There wasn't very many of those lumberjacks deformed, but this guy had a Crooked Nick, but he was pretty good at work. Didn't affect his work. No. You know, they didn't hold back punches. If some deformer here and a guy, he got that name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay, what about Joe Takeachance?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh yeah. Anything to do asking to do, alright, I'll take a chance. Yeah, I'll take a chance. Yeah, okay, alright, I'll take a chance. Push it, say you won't take a chance, you'll do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was his luck?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, they take it in. Sounds like he thought he was... Good working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then this Dirty Shirt John, is that that same guy you were telling me about before? Or is that another guy?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Dirty, well he always wore a dirty shirt. Like I said, they'd nickname him something...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dirty Shirt John?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: There's a Dirty Bert, I'll give you that, he was a cook and he was dirty. Dirty Bert, B -E -R -T. First name was Bert, and then he was dirty in the kitchen. Dirty Bert, call him Dirty Bert.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was his food?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, I was dirty too, he didn't last long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay, what about Broomface?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Okay, he was the river driver, he was boss of the river driver. Broomface Brooks. And when the weather got cold, I didn't think it would go right on the river drive. His face looked like it got that much longer, about the shape of a broom. I think he did too now. Broomface Brooks.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He was push on the river drive.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many of these guys that were jacks up around Bowville also get in on those river drives?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah, they'd blow over there. You know, down around here, up in this country, Longing started first in and went down over to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this Ridge Runner?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I've heard about this guy. Alright. He wasn't such a bad guy. Well, he wasn't an awful big man either, working the woods. And when the war came on, men were scarce. And he'd go around all these forestry camps, closed up, you know. And he'd break in and live there for a while, eat the grub, and he'd go to another one, break in and eat the grub. And I knew him. I can't think of his name right now. I knew him. I know one day, the head forestry come down there. I was working, framing poles, and there were about ten men working there. And he come down and he asked the superintendent, he said, I'd like to get some of your men to go with me and hunt up this Ridge Runner. Well, the guy said, well, that's alright with me, but you'll have to go and see them if they won't say no. I said, yes, fine, Danny. Well, he come down amongst us and I talked to this ranger. I told him, I said, I know quite a bit about forest supervisors. And I said, of course, the bow pit I was with, there was a few rangers, but the forest supervisor was rangers. He says, well, I said, I know that ranger, and I said, he's not hurting anybody. His grub is only stealing nothing else. He's probably hungry. And so, a little paper boy right there, go and read today's paper if you would like.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And he kind of, and when he heard me talk to the other guys, nobody went out with him. He wasn't hurting anybody, he was getting a little grub off them. And then after a while, they blamed him for blowing up a cat, but he didn't blow it up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like to talk to? What? Who would like to talk to? What was he like, the Ridge Runner?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He worked there after that, sometime after that, he worked there, and he was alright, fine. I had him rolling poles and things like that. He was fine. But I don't think they brought that charge against him about setting fires with a cat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Bull O 'Keefe?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Alright, Bull O 'Keefe. He, uh, but, uh, thanks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'll make you go for me, will you?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Bull O 'Keefe, he was a native of Bovill, and he had one, two, three, four, five daughters, as pretty as can be. And the last was twins, a boy and a girl. The last two were twins of a boy and a girl. And he had, he was, uh, load logs. Why, hello.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You ready to mow the lawn?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No. Well, he, uh, he was kind of tough on the guy. You know, they take, they got gippled loading the logs, and he'd be the head man loading the logs. And they'd done it by contract. And, uh, he was rough on the crew they worked with. So they called him Bull O 'Keefe. But he always got, uh, they're all married now, and married well. And Jack O 'Keefe, you know, the last two were twins, and he's got the wholesale oil outfit here in Bovill and Marfino too. The Shell. A great friend of mine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Silent Joe?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, yeah. And Silent Joe. He's, uh, he goes for weeks and never hardly say a word. And then he opened up for about two or three days and never stopped talking.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: You couldn't get rid of him when he stopped talking, when he was talked. And he was only glad the time come back when he was silent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he talk about, do you remember?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: When he was silent, nothing. No, when he was talking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he talk about himself or about the world?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Anything he could think of. He was probably intelligent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Walking daily?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Walking daily.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that daily?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: The kind of thing I'm wrong about, walking daily. There's another name there, not daily.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: The other guy I'm thinking of now, daily don't belong in there. Walking is something else, I can't think of it. Well, that's okay. Well, now I know. He'd walk up and down in the bunkhouse when the others was asleep.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And when he'd come to town like, you know, he'd walk down the middle of the road at night.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: But daily, there's another word not daily in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Cruel Jimmy?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, that's Cruel Jimmy Holmes. That was his name, Jimmy Holmes. H -O -L -M -E -S. And he was cruel. He had a bunch of men working for him and boy, he worked the dickens out of him. There's another one of these tough foremen. Yeah. Cruel Jimmy Holmes. He must have been pretty bad if they called him cruel. Yeah. Of course, if he wasn't quite so cruel, they'd call him cruel anyhow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sleigh Hall Brown?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: All right. Sleigh Hall Brown. He always wanted to be in with Sleigh Hall. You know, there's not much sleigh hauling in this country. But in the 20s, 21, 22, they'd dump some sleigh hauling around. Well, the way it was,

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: a lot of these outfits leased farmers and things like that. Round Helmer, that's where her uncle's name, Helmer. Around there, they leased land and the state leased the land to them but gave them a certain time to get off it. And it's because the potlites wanted to buy the timber. Well, the potlites bought all the timber they could. Some of them wouldn't sell it to them. And that was the last sleigh hauling, as Sleigh Hall Brown did. So we called him Sleigh Hall Bill, Sleigh Hall Brown. So they had to do a sleigh haul because they had to get the timber out before the lease was up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that right?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Before the lease was up, they had to get the timber out. And some of the farmers wouldn't give them any extension, and a lot did. Well, some of the guys that did give them an extension, give the company an extension, they even left the timber on there. And got it off these other farmers, and quick, done everything to get it off. They called that the Park Sleigh Hall out in Helmer. That's the Park Sleigh Hall, 1922.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's this guy that's cold ham?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He was a cook. He lays here all his eat -servers cold ham. Breakfast, dinner, and supper. Cold ham with Snyder.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Cold ham with cider?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Snyder.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, Snyder?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And then there's a guy called Cannonball.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Cannonball?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: All right. His name was Ed...I think it was only after a while. He was an engineer on a logging locomotive. And he was always using the word cannonball. And this time he tried to cut a bunch of flats on the hill. He didn't hook up with them, and gee, down the road he went.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Wrecked. Cannonball. I think he was named in the minute.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Cannonball means the guy's a high roller, right?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He's fast. Yeah, old Cannonball. Fast roller.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, you tell me when we think his name later. I want to ask you about P .V. Jerry, too. Oh, I saw him a couple of weeks ago in Lova. I drove up with Phil to go up to Bovine, Elk River, to look at some insurance deals. And that Jerry Carlin, he came out and saw us. He came out and we stood by there, talking through the window to us about an hour. It was a car. Laughing all the time, we're telling jokes about the guy's... He's very witty.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where does he live now?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: In Bovine, Bovine, Bovine, Bovine, Bovine, Bovine. Now, if you're going up that way, you put his name down, Jerry Carlin. So why do they call him P .V.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jerry?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, you don't like to be called it, but anyhow, he had a P .V. And those lumberjacks kept the P .V. in perfect shape. They'd have a piece of glass and shave it off, you know, and polish it, and have this just right so they could handle it. Just like a violinist with his violin. So they called him P .V. Jerry. And now, put his name down there. I did. Alright. And put Teddy Carlin down there.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, he kept his P .V. in good shape, is that why you called him P .V.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jerry?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Alright, now on that last Carlin, see his wife, B .E .A. B. Carlin, B .E .A. And boy, she'll give you a lot of dope. She writes for the Lewis and Paver.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where does she live?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: She lives in a house I used to live in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Beauvoir?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: The last house on the left is going towards Elk River.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's the White House, right in town, see?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: It's got round pillars up in the middle of it, you know, like those old... three or four to hold. It's overhanging, and a room is upside down, and that pillar is up there to hold it up. Nice house. And B, her name is B. Oh, she seems pretty good.
